EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across northern portions of the forecast area was the focus for afternoon convection on the afternoon of June 23rd. A weak shortwave swinging in from the west helped to increase wind fields aloft and strong heating south of the front produced CAPES in the 1000 to 1500 j/kg range. Large hail and damaging winds were the main threats with these storms, the stronger of which held together long enough to drift into southern portions of the region later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 0.75 inch hail near Fryeburg.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm snapped off several large trees about 20 ft off the ground in Fryeburg near the Lovell town line. Hail less then 1 inch in diameter was also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wavering stationary front was situated northwest of the forecast area on the afternoon of June 29th. South of this boundary high values of precipitable water and very steep low level lapse rates combined with good shear to produce diurnally driven organized convection which initiated in New Hampshire and became severe as it drifted into western Maine. Large hail and damaging winds were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 0.88 inch hail near Carthage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front produced thunderstorms which were training over northern Franklin and Somerset Counties. These storms produced over 4 inches of rain in a brief period of time resulting in flooded roads and road washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of training thunderstorms produced over 4 inches of rain washing out Long Falls Dam Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front was the focus for thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall on the afternoon of June 26th. Numerous road washouts and flooding were reported in Franklin and Somerset Counties. Damage estimates topped 175,000.00 in the two counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing very heavy rain dropped 2 to 4 inches in less than 3 hours resulting in numerous reports of road washouts near Avon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front was the focus for thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall on the afternoon of June 26th. Numerous road washouts and flooding were reported in Franklin and Somerset Counties. Damage estimates topped 175,000.00 in the two counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall dropped 2 to 4 inches in less than 3 hours resulting in numerous road washouts northeast of Eustis. King and Bartlett Road was down to one lane in places. Severe damage was reported around Camera Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached Maine during the afternoon of June 2nd...while a pre-frontal trof and upper level disturbance crossed the region. Severe thunderstorms developed across northern and eastern Maine during the early afternoon hours of the 2nd...then persisted into the evening. Some locations experienced several rounds of severe weather through the afternoon and evening with damaging winds and large hail. Heavy rain produced flash flooding in spots...particularly in areas which experienced several thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were toppled...along with downed power lines...by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure over the southeastern states on the morning of the 29th moved rapidly north-northeastward to Nova Scotia by the morning of the 30th.  The low brought abundant moisture northward and produced 6 to 14 inches of snow across the interior and foothill sections of western Maine.  Along the coast, warmer temperatures caused the precipitation to change to rain.

Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary during the evening of the 30th. Training thunderstorms produced localized flash flooding across portions of Piscataquis county starting during the late evening hours. The flash flooding persisted into the early morning hours of July 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms with rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour produced flash flooding in the Monson area. Route 6/15 was damaged in several locations due to washouts and shoulder damage. The flash flooding lasted into the early morning hours of July 1st.

Some locations experienced several rounds of severe weather through the afternoon and evening with damaging winds and large hail. Heavy rain produced flash flooding in spots...particularly in areas which experienced several thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled near Allagash Falls by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across northern portions of the forecast area was the focus for afternoon convection on the afternoon of June 23rd. A weak shortwave swinging in from the west helped to increase wind fields aloft and strong heating south of the front produced CAPES in the 1000 to 1500 j/kg range. Large hail and damaging winds were the main threats with these storms, the stronger of which held together long enough to drift into southern portions of the region later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on a road in Wells.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wavering stationary front was situated northwest of the forecast area on the afternoon of June 29th. South of this boundary high values of precipitable water and very steep low level lapse rates combined with good shear to produce diurnally driven organized convection which initiated in New Hampshire and became severe as it drifted into western Maine. Large hail and damaging winds were associated with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees near Carthage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front was the focus for thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall on the afternoon of June 26th. Numerous road washouts and flooding were reported in Franklin and Somerset Counties. Damage estimates topped 175,000.00 in the two counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms producing very heavy rainfall dropped 2 to 4 inches in less than three hours in the Phillips area of Franklin County. Bog Pond and Pinkham Hill Roads were undermined by running water. Scammon Road had one lane washed out. Fish Hatchery Road had 400 feet of pavement removed by rushing water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across northern portions of the forecast area was the focus for afternoon convection on the afternoon of June 23rd. A weak shortwave swinging in from the west helped to increase wind fields aloft and strong heating south of the front produced CAPES in the 1000 to 1500 j/kg range. Large hail and damaging winds were the main threats with these storms, the stronger of which held together long enough to drift into southern portions of the region later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 25-yr-old woman was injured in a lightning strike at Point Sebago Resort.  The woman was standing near a tall pine at the edge of Sebago Lake when lightning struck the tree.  She was reportedly thrown into the air by the strike and was unconscious after landing on  some rocks and a concrete barrier.  She was taken to a nearby hospital for evaluation and treatment.  Two others who were also reportedly thrown into the air were not injured.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A teenage boy was struck by lightning inside his home.  The lightning reportedly shot across the room, although no details were available concerning what the boy was doing at the time.  He was transported to a nearby hospital for treatment and observation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of September 12th. Strong shear was present as a cold front moved into the region from the west during the early afternoon. Large hail and damaging winds were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced 1 to 2 inch hail in Upton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in the Bethel area. Heavy rain caused the Pleasant River to flood Meadowbrook Bridge Road and Flat Road in Bethel.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in 4 hours causing the Pleasant River to flood Meadow Bridge Road and Flat Road in Bethel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of slow moving thunderstorm dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in 3 hours causing the Noisy Brook to rise rapidly and flood Route 17 in Roxbury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of slow moving thunderstorms dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in 3 hours causing Noisy Brook to rise rapidly and flood Route 17 in Roxbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours of the 3rd in advance of a cold front crossing the region...then persisted into the evening hours. A few of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were toppled or snapped along a 7 mile stretch of Katahdin Iron Works Road by wind gusts estimated at 70 mph. The damage occurred between Gulf Hagas and Katahdin Iron Works.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours of the 3rd in advance of a cold front crossing the region...then persisted into the evening hours. A few of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported at Jo-Mary campground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours of the 3rd in advance of a cold front crossing the region...then persisted into the evening hours. A few of the storms became severe producing damaging winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled along Route 2...between Island Falls and Sherman...by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The trees blocked one lane of the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon and evening of August 28th. Although shear was weak across the region, a sea breeze front pushed inland during the late afternoon and early evening producing enough forcing to initiate convection in the foothills of western Maine. High precipitable water values resulted in slow moving thunderstorms with very heavy rain, some of which produced wet microbursts. Thunderstorm wind damage and some flash flooding were reported with stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large trees across Route 201 between Bingham and Caratunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure began to build across the region from the overnight hours of the 30th into the 31st. This was the start of an extended interval of extremely cold temperatures which persisted into early January. Overnight low temperatures from the 30th into the 31st fell to 10 to 20 below zero across northern areas...with single digits below zero Downeast. High temperatures on the 31st struggled to rise above zero across northern areas...with generally single digits above zero Downeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended interval of extremely cold temperatures began late December and persisted into early January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking northeast across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow and gusty winds to the region. Snow developed during the early morning hours of the 15th. Snow...heavy at times...then continued across central and Downeast areas through the afternoon hours. The heavy snow persisted across northern areas into the early morning hours of the 16th. Warnings verified through the morning into the early afternoon hours of the 15th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 18 inches across northern and central areas with 8 to 13 inches Downeast. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph produced near blizzard conditions at times along the Downeast coast with extensive blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 14 inches. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph produced near blizzard conditions at times with extensive blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several weak areas of low pressure tracked across Downeast Maine along a nearly stationary frontal boundary through the 21st into the 22nd. Precipitation began during the afternoon of the 21st then continued into the afternoon of the 22nd. Across east-central and interior Downeast areas precipitation was mostly in the form of sleet and  freezing rain. Through the afternoon of the 22nd total precipitation in these areas generally ranged from 1 to 3 inches of sleet along with .10 to .50 inch of ice. During this same time...precipitation along the Downeast coast was mostly in the form of freezing rain with ice accumulations of .25 to .50 inch. Warnings verified through the morning into the early afternoon hours of the 22nd. This was the first portion of a major icing event across Downeast Maine. |Power outages began to develop during the overnight hours of the 21st into the morning of the 22nd. Power outages during the first portion of the event peaked at around 20000 customers early on the 22nd...but had fallen to around 8000 later on the 22nd.  The most extensive power outages during this first portion of the event were across coastal areas of Hancock and Washington counties. Numerous snapped branches and ice weighted trees making contact with power lines contributed to power outages. The power outages and damage to trees became much more extensive during the second portion of the event.  |The final in the series of weak lows drew the frontal boundary southward across the Gulf of Maine during the afternoon of the 22nd which helped reinforce the low level cold air. Precipitation generally tapered to freezing drizzle in the wake of the final low which persisted through the evening. However...this set the stage for the second portion of the event which occurred from the overnight hours of the 22nd through the 23rd when a stronger low moved across the Gulf of Maine along the frontal boundary. This low brought additional significant freezing rain to Downeast areas. The second portion of the event is addressed in greater detail in a seperate write-up. The overall combined impacts of both events are adressed in greater detail with the write-up for the second portion of the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total sleet accumulations ranged from 1 to 3 inches. Storm total ice accumulations ranged from a tenth to a quarter of an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure began to build across the region from the overnight hours of the 30th into the 31st. This was the start of an extended interval of extremely cold temperatures which persisted into early January. Overnight low temperatures from the 30th into the 31st fell to 10 to 20 below zero across northern areas...with single digits below zero Downeast. High temperatures on the 31st struggled to rise above zero across northern areas...with generally single digits above zero Downeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended interval of extremely cold temperatures began late December and persisted into early January.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking northeast across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow and gusty winds to the region. Snow developed during the early morning hours of the 15th. Snow...heavy at times...then continued across central and Downeast areas through the afternoon hours. The heavy snow persisted across northern areas into the early morning hours of the 16th. Warnings verified through the morning into the early afternoon hours of the 15th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 18 inches across northern and central areas with 8 to 13 inches Downeast. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph produced near blizzard conditions at times along the Downeast coast with extensive blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 13 inches. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph produced near blizzard conditions at times with extensive blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked quickly across the Gulf of Maine through the night of the 29th into the morning of the 30th. The low brought heavy snow to portions of Downeast and east-central Maine. Snow developed during the afternoon of the 29th and continued into the early morning hours of the 30th. Warnings verified between 9 PM and midnight on the 29th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 9 to 14 inches...with local totals up to around 16 inches. Although the storm was fast moving...snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour were reported at times contributing to the snow totals. Lesser snow accumulations occurred along the Downeast coast where precipitation transitioned from snow to a mix of sleet...freezing rain and rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 9 to 15 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain developed along a nearly stationary frontal boundary during the evening of June 30th. Training thunderstorms produced localized flash flooding across portions of Piscataquis county starting during the late evening hours. The flash flooding persisted into the early morning hours of the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms with rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour produced flash flooding in the Monson area. Route 6/15 was damaged in several locations due to washouts and shoulder damage. This was a continuation of flash flooding which began during the evening of June 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper disturbance crossed the region during the afternoon and evening hours of the 17th. Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Maine in advance of the front during the late afternoon hours...then persisted into the evening. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds...along with a brief tornado in the Danforth area.  Heavy rain produced localized flash flooding in the Caribou area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two power lines were toppled near Connor by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second portion of a major ice storm event across Downeast Maine occurred from the overnight hours of the 22nd through the 23rd. Low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine along a nearly stationary front brought additional freezing rain of varying intensities to Downeast Maine from the overnight hours of the 22nd into the evening of the 23rd. Warnings for this portion of the event verified around noon on the 23rd.  Additional ice accumulation during this portion of the event generally ranged from .25 to .50 inch. This was in addition to the .10 to .50 inch of ice which had occurred less than 24 hours earlier. Total ice accumulations across Downeast areas from both portions of the event generally ranged from .50 to .75 inch...with localized totals around 1.00 inch. The greatest ice accumulations generally occurred across Hancock county along with central and coastal portions of Washington county.|The impacts of the ice and extent of power outages increased significantly from the overnight hours of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. During this time the ice began to bring down trees on power lines along with the snapped branches and weighted branches making contact with the lines. The weight of the ice on power lines also began to snap utility poles during this time. Fallen trees...snapped branches and ice accumulation made some roads nearly impassable which slowed the rate of power recovery. Cold temperatures in the wake of the event also slowed recovery efforts. At the maximum extent...early on the 23rd...40000 to 50000 customers were estimated to be without power. The most severely impacted area was Hancock county where some customers were without power for nearly a week. The Governor declared a State of Emergency which included Downeast areas. Emergency shelters were opened across the region to accomodate those without power. Power crews from across the northeast were brought in to help with the power restoration. Impacts from this event rivaled those of the historic ice storm of 1998 in some areas. This was the most extensive damage to the power grid in some areas since the ice storm of 1998.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total ice accumulations ranged from a quarter to a half inch. This was in addition to the tenth to quarter of an inch of ice which had occurred less than 24 hours earlier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second portion of a major ice storm event across Downeast Maine occurred from the overnight hours of the 22nd through the 23rd. Low pressure tracking across the Gulf of Maine along a nearly stationary front brought additional freezing rain of varying intensities to Downeast Maine from the overnight hours of the 22nd into the evening of the 23rd. Warnings for this portion of the event verified around noon on the 23rd.  Additional ice accumulation during this portion of the event generally ranged from .25 to .50 inch. This was in addition to the .10 to .50 inch of ice which had occurred less than 24 hours earlier. Total ice accumulations across Downeast areas from both portions of the event generally ranged from .50 to .75 inch...with localized totals around 1.00 inch. The greatest ice accumulations generally occurred across Hancock county along with central and coastal portions of Washington county.|The impacts of the ice and extent of power outages increased significantly from the overnight hours of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. During this time the ice began to bring down trees on power lines along with the snapped branches and weighted branches making contact with the lines. The weight of the ice on power lines also began to snap utility poles during this time. Fallen trees...snapped branches and ice accumulation made some roads nearly impassable which slowed the rate of power recovery. Cold temperatures in the wake of the event also slowed recovery efforts. At the maximum extent...early on the 23rd...40000 to 50000 customers were estimated to be without power. The most severely impacted area was Hancock county where some customers were without power for nearly a week. The Governor declared a State of Emergency which included Downeast areas. Emergency shelters were opened across the region to accomodate those without power. Power crews from across the northeast were brought in to help with the power restoration. Impacts from this event rivaled those of the historic ice storm of 1998 in some areas. This was the most extensive damage to the power grid in some areas since the ice storm of 1998.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total ice accumulations ranged from a quarter to a half inch. This was in addition to the tenth to quarter of an inch of ice which had occurred less than 24 hours earlier.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm developed across southern Penobscot county during the evening of the 18th along a neary stationary frontal boundary. The thunderstorm produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and utility poles were toppled by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph. Some trees fell on cars. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon hours of the 19th in advance of an approaching cold front. The storms then persisted into the evening. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds. A tornado also occurred in northern Baxter State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with estimated wind gusts up to 75 mph produced around an 18 mile swath of tree damage...up to 1000 feet wide at times...across northern Piscataquis county. Most of this path crossed northern portions of Baxter State Park. Along the swath...widespread tree damage occurred with trees uprooted and snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracking northeast across the Gulf of Maine brought heavy snow and gusty winds to the region. Snow developed during the early morning hours of the 15th. Snow...heavy at times...then continued across central and Downeast areas through the afternoon hours. The heavy snow persisted across northern areas into the early morning hours of the 16th. Warnings verified through the morning into the early afternoon hours of the 15th. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 18 inches across northern and central areas with 8 to 13 inches Downeast. Wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph produced near blizzard conditions at times along the Downeast coast with extensive blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked quickly across the Gulf of Maine through the night of the 29th into the morning of the 30th. The low brought heavy snow to portions of Downeast and east-central Maine. Snow developed during the afternoon of the 29th and continued into the early morning hours of the 30th. Warnings verified between 9 PM and midnight on the 29th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 9 to 14 inches...with local totals up to around 16 inches. Although the storm was fast moving...snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour were reported at times contributing to the snow totals. Lesser snow accumulations occurred along the Downeast coast where precipitation transitioned from snow to a mix of sleet...freezing rain and rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

Central Maine Power reported that about 48,500 customers lost electrical service with nearly 50 poles snapped by falling trees and branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Arctic high pressure began to build across the region from the overnight hours of the 30th into the 31st. This was the start of an extended interval of extremely cold temperatures which persisted into early January. Overnight low temperatures from the 30th into the 31st fell to 10 to 20 below zero across northern areas...with single digits below zero Downeast. High temperatures on the 31st struggled to rise above zero across northern areas...with generally single digits above zero Downeast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An extended interval of extremely cold temperatures began late December and persisted into early January.

The highest astronomical tides of the month (11.8 feet in Portland Harbor) combined with a weak, but developing, area of low pressure (1000 mb) near Cape Cod to produce coastal flooding in the region during the late evening hours of May 25th.|Fortunately winds were relatively light, with relatively modest seas along the coast. A storm surge of 0.87 feet in Portland Harbor brought the storm tide to 12.61 feet. This resulted in minor coastal flooding along the wharfs and adjacent side streets in Portland. Granite Point and Biddeford Pool Roads were covered in water in Biddeford, and flooding occurred in a parking lot at the City Landing in Belfast. Minor erosion and splash-over occurred along the beach roads in Kennebunk, Wells and York. Additional flooding may have occurred along the coast, however due to the late night timing of the storm tide, little was observed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with moderate seas and developing low pressure to produce coastal flooding in Portland. Flooding occurred along the wharfs and adjacent side streets in Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave trough and an associated cold front approached the forecast area on the afternoon of June 17th. Moderate to strong speed shear shifted into southern Maine by mid afternoon as CAPE values peaked. Several severe thunderstorms developed during the remainder of the afternoon with wind damage the main feature with these storms. Some small hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on wires which caught fire on Rudolph Avenue in Kittery.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled along Route 161 from just north of New Sweden to Cross Lake by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph. The fallen trees blocked portions of the road for a time.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Seven trees were toppled along Richardson Road near Castle Hill by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph. The trees toppled were up to 2 feet in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south across northern Maine during the afternoon hours of the 31st...with thunderstorms developing in advance of the front. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds across portions of northern Aroostook county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking along a slow moving cold front produced 1.5 to 3 inches of rain across northern and central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding on the Kennebec River at Skowhegan (flood stage 35,000 cfs) which crested at 48,588 cfs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south across northern Maine during the afternoon hours of the 31st...with thunderstorms developing in advance of the front. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds across portions of northern Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A second severe thunderstorm crossed Fort Kent during the late afternoon hours. Several  trees were again toppled around town by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. Scattered power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached Maine during the afternoon of June 2nd...while a pre-frontal trof and upper level disturbance crossed the region. Severe thunderstorms developed across northern and eastern Maine during the early afternoon hours of the 2nd...then persisted into the evening. Some locations experienced several rounds of severe weather through the afternoon and evening with damaging winds and large hail. Heavy rain produced flash flooding in spots...particularly in areas which experienced several thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst toppled numerous large trees along Route 11 and Sly Brook Road. Winds with the microburst were estimated at 65 mph. A gustnado also briefly developed along the leading edge of the microburst. The vast majority of the tree damage was due to winds from the microburst. The falling trees broke windows at one camp house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved south across northern Maine during the afternoon hours of the 31st...with thunderstorms developing in advance of the front. Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds across portions of northern Aroostook county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled along Route 162 near Sinclair by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph. The fallen trees blocked portions of Route 162...along with many dirt forest roads in the area...for a time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking along a slow moving cold front produced 1.5 to 3 inches of rain across northern and central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced flooding on the Kennebec River at Augusta (flood stage 12.0 ft) which crested at 13.33 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking along a slow moving cold front produced 1.5 to 3 inches of rain across northern and central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain produced minor flooding on the Kennebec River at North Sidney (flood stage 17.0 ft) which crested at 17.02 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the region during the day on the 19th of July, leaving the forecast area in a very hot and humid air mass. A few thunderstorms along this boundary became severe during the morning hours but bulk of the severe storms came during the evening hours. Thunderstorms riding over an upper level ridge along this boundary developed during the late afternoon across eastern Ontario and southern Quebec. These storm intensified as they dropped southeast through the evening hours. Damage from these storms was exclusively due to strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees near Rockwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large Bermuda High anchored off the east coast created a very hot and moist southwest flow into New England on the 5th of July. Isolated air mass type thunderstorms developed during the evening hours. One of these storms became severe downing numerous trees in a Freeport campground.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed 75 to 100 trees at Recompence Campground in Freeport. Several cars and campers were damaged by the falling trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass remained entrenched across the forecast area on the 20th of July. An approaching cold front set the stage for more severe thunderstorms across the region. Once again elevated freezing levels inhibited development of large hail and all reports of severe weather involved wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Sidney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave and associated cold front were approaching from the northwest during the late afternoon and evening hours of July 17th. Very high precipitable water values and very warm temperatures ahead of the front combined with increasing shear to produce numerous severe thunderstorms, mainly during the evening hours. Due to high freezing levels, wind damage and very heavy rain were the main features of these storms as they moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees in Whitefield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary across northern portions of the forecast area was the focus for afternoon convection on the afternoon of June 23rd. A weak shortwave swinging in from the west helped to increase wind fields aloft and strong heating south of the front produced CAPES in the 1000 to 1500 j/kg range. Large hail and damaging winds were the main threats with these storms, the stronger of which held together long enough to drift into southern portions of the region later in the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on a house on Thompson Point Road in Naples.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with strong wind fields and high CAPES over the region resulting in a widespread severe thunderstorm outbreak during the afternoon and evening hours of June 2nd. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced microburst damage in a remote area off Meader Farm Road in Solon.  The damage area was about a half mile long and about 300 yards wide.  Several hundred trees were snapped or uprooted by winds that were estimated to have gusted between 70 and 80 mph.

These storms produced mainly wind damage with isolated reports of large hail and some flash flooding.  A weak tornado touched down in Pleasant Ridge Plantation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree and wires in Brighton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with strong wind fields and high CAPES over the region resulting in a widespread severe thunderstorm outbreak during the afternoon and evening hours of June 2nd. These storms produced mainly wind damage with isolated reports of large hail and some flash flooding.  A weak tornado touched down in Pleasant Ridge Plantation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A very weak tornado touched down near Pleasant Ridge Road in Pleasant Ridge Plantation.  Maximum wind gusts were estimated at between 50 and 60 mph.  Damage from the tornado was in a small area estimated to have a path length of 50-60 yards with a width of 40 yards. Damage was limited to about a dozen snapped or uprooted trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with strong wind fields and high CAPES over the region resulting in a widespread severe thunderstorm outbreak during the afternoon and evening hours of June 2nd. These storms produced mainly wind damage with isolated reports of large hail and some flash flooding.  A weak tornado touched down in Pleasant Ridge Plantation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A macroburst from a severe thunderstorm snapped or uprooted an estimated several thousand trees in the town of Rangeley.  The damage area was about 7.5 miles long and about 3.6 miles wide. Falling trees blocked roads and damaged vehicles in the area.  The strongest winds were estimated to be between 70 and 80 mph near Haines Landing on the east side of Mooselookmeguntic Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracking northeast along the Saint Lawrence River Valley drew a cold front across the region through the morning and afternoon hours of the 31st. A strong low level jet moved across the region in advance of the front. South to southwest winds gusted to 50 to 60 mph at times in advance of the front across much of the region inland from the coast...with 60 to 70 mph gusts along the Downeast coast.|The winds snapped branches and brought down numerous trees across Downeast areas which contributed to extensive power outages. At the time of peak outages...during the late afternoon and evening...nearly 23000 customers were without power Downeast. Numerous trees were reported down in Bangor...Springfield...Burlington...Orono...Corrina and Corinth along with towns along the coast. The metal roof was torn from a structure in Old Town. Lesser tree damage and power outages were reported across northern areas. A narrow line of convectively enhanced winds...without lightning...also crossed the region in advance of the cold front. This line produced localized wind damage around Easton... in northeast Aroostook county...where an empty tractor trailer was blown over and a garage lost its roof. The winds entered the garage through an open door...lifting the roof from the structure. Scattered power outages were reported across northern Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong south to southwest winds occurred in advance of a cold front crossing the region. Wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were common. The winds snapped branches and brought down many trees contributing to extensive power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm located over the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 14th moved northeast during the day.  A secondary low developed off the Mid-Atlantic coast and intensified rapidly by the morning of the 15th.  The secondary low continued to intensify as it moved through the Gulf of Maine during the morning of the 15th and into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 16th.  The low spread heavy snow across all of western Maine with amounts generally ranging from 8 to 14 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm affected the State of Maine from December 21st, 2013, through January 6th,, 2014.  A mixture of precipitation fell across the state from December 21st through the 23rd with snow across northern areas, a mixture of snow and sleet across north central areas, a mixture of sleet and freezing rain across south central areas, and a mixture of freezing rain and rain in southern areas.  Due to the weight of the ice on tree branches and limbs from freezing rain, widespread power outages occurred in portions southern and central Maine.  Because subfreezing temperatures followed the storm and persisted for about two weeks, the ice remained on the trees for a prolonged period of time.  During this two-week period, snow from a series of storms hampered clean-up efforts and added weight to the branches and limbs that were already coated with ice which caused more power outages.  In addition, extreme cold temperatures and wind chills hampered restoration efforts.||The prolonged precipitation event occurred as a frontal boundary remained south of the state and an area of arctic high pressure to the north funneled cold air southwestward across the State.  At the same time, southerly winds aloft brought abundant moisture into the State as a series of disturbances moved northeastward along the frontal boundary.  During the period of December 21 -23, northern areas of the State saw up to 10 inches of snow and sleet with many southern areas seeing from one 1/2 to 3/4 inches of ice accretion.  A few spots reported about 1 inch of ice.   ||The weight of the ice and later the weight of ice and snow resulted in falling debris which caused scattered to widespread power outages across the state.  Some of the power outages lasted more than a week.   Although the event in southern Oxford, southern Franklin, southern Somerset and interior Cumberland Counties didn't qualify as an ices storm based on coverage, portions of these counties had between a half and three quarters of an inch of ice accretion which led to downed trees and power outages in the affected areas.  In addition, gusty winds during the period also caused additional outages due to falling ice-laden branches.  Central Maine Power reported 127,000 customers that lost power from the 22nd to the 28th, another 26,000 customers that lost power from the 29th through the 31st, and about 5000 customers that lost electrical service from the storm on the 3rd.    Bangor Hydro/EMERA reported a peak outage of 39,000 customers on the 23rd with several thousand more outages on the 26thand 28th, and fewer outages on the 3rd and 6th.  Eastern Maine Electric Co-op reported about 5000 outages late in the day on the 23rd.||The combination of warmer weather and gusty winds on January 6th finally melted all the ice off trees throughout the state, ending the effects of the December 21-23 ice storm.

A mixture of precipitation fell across the state from December 21st through the 23rd with snow across northern areas, a mixture of snow and sleet across north central areas, a mixture of sleet and freezing rain across south central areas, and a mixture of freezing rain and rain in southern areas.  Due to the weight of the ice on tree branches and limbs from freezing rain, widespread power outages occurred in portions southern and central Maine.  Because subfreezing temperatures followed the storm and persisted for about two weeks, the ice remained on the trees for a prolonged period of time.  During this two-week period, snow from a series of storms hampered clean-up efforts and added weight to the branches and limbs that were already coated with ice which caused more power outages.  In addition, extreme cold temperatures and wind chills hampered restoration efforts.||The prolonged precipitation event occurred as a frontal boundary remained south of the state and an area of arctic high pressure to the north funneled cold air southwestward across the State.  At the same time, southerly winds aloft brought abundant moisture into the State as a series of disturbances moved northeastward along the frontal boundary.  During the period of December 21 -23, northern areas of the State saw up to 10 inches of snow and sleet with many southern areas seeing from one 1/2 to 3/4 inches of ice accretion.  A few spots reported about 1 inch of ice.   ||The weight of the ice and later the weight of ice and snow resulted in falling debris which caused scattered to widespread power outages across the state.  Some of the power outages lasted more than a week.   Although the event in southern Oxford, southern Franklin, southern Somerset and interior Cumberland Counties didn't qualify as an ices storm based on coverage, portions of these counties had between a half and three quarters of an inch of ice accretion which led to downed trees and power outages in the affected areas.  In addition, gusty winds during the period also caused additional outages due to falling ice-laden branches.  Central Maine Power reported 127,000 customers that lost power from the 22nd to the 28th, another 26,000 customers that lost power from the 29th through the 31st, and about 5000 customers that lost electrical service from the storm on the 3rd.    Bangor Hydro/EMERA reported a peak outage of 39,000 customers on the 23rd with several thousand more outages on the 26thand 28th, and fewer outages on the 3rd and 6th.  Eastern Maine Electric Co-op reported about 5000 outages late in the day on the 23rd.||The combination of warmer weather and gusty winds on January 6th finally melted all the ice off trees throughout the state, ending the effects of the December 21-23 ice storm.

Intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine during the overnight hours of the 19th into the 20th. The low then lifted northeast across New Brunswick during the 20th.  The low brought heavy snow to northern and eastern Maine. Snow expanded northeast across the region through the late morning and afternoon hours of the 19th. Snow...heavy at times...then occurred through the evening hours of the 19th into the morning of the 20th. Warning criteria snow totals were met through the evening hours of the 19th into the early morning hours of the 20th. The snow wound down across much of the region through the morning of the 20th...lingering across extreme northern and northeastern areas into the early afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations across Downeast areas generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches...with localized totals up to 15 inches. Storm totals across central and northern areas generally ranged from 9 to 15 inches...with localized totals up to 19 inches. The heaviest accumulations generally occurred from northern Penobscot to southeast Aroostook counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 12 to 17 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed rapidly off the Carolina coast late on the 7th and early on the 8th.  The storm moved very slowly northeast during the 8th and 9th as it continued to intensify.  By the morning of the 10th, the storm was located just to the east of Nova Scotia.  The storm brought heavy snow, high winds, and blizzard conditions to the state.  Snowfall amounts were generally 18 inches or more in coastal and interior zones where blizzard conditions caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.  In the mountains and foothills, snowfall amounts were in the 4 to 18 inch range.  Most of coastal Maine had blizzard conditions for 9 to 12 hours with interior areas reporting blizzard conditions for 3 to 10 hours.

A slow moving frontal boundary across northern Maine helped focus deep Atlantic moisture and strong lift across the region. Snow developed through the late morning and early afternoon hours of February 28th. Slow moving bands of heavy snow then produced significant accumulations across portions of northern and northeastern Maine through the remainder of the afternoon hours of the 28th into the early morning hours of March 1st. Snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour were reported at times along with thunder snow during the evening hours. Warning criteria snow totals were met through the evening hours of the 28th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 8 to 18 inches...with a localized total up to 21 inches near Fort Fairfield in northeast Aroostook county. The banded structure of the heavier snow contributed to the variability of snow totals. A record daily snow total of 13.6 inches was set at Caribou...in northeast Aroostook county...on the 28th. The snow ended during the early morning hours of the 1st. This was a continuation of an event which had begun on February 28th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 11 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dangerous wind chills of 35 to 40 below zero occurred through the early morning and morning hours of the 5th.

Rapidly intensifying low pressure tracked from the southeast coast to a location southeast of Cape Cod during the night of the 16th. The low further intensified while tracking to Nova Scotia during the 17th. Blizzard conditions developed through the afternoon and evening hours of the 17th. |Storm total snow accumulations during the blizzard generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches across northern areas...with 4 to 6 inches Downeast. This was in addition to 4 to 7 inches of snow which had fallen across the region within the previous 12 hours from another storm. Wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common across the region...with occasional gusts to around 55 mph...producing extensive blowing and drifting snow along with whiteout conditions. The tight pressure gradient between the intense low across Newfoundland and high pressure to the west kept strong gusty winds across the region into the 18th. Blizzard conditions diminished through the morning hours of the 18th...though gusty winds produced areas of blowing snow across eastern areas into the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 6 inches...along with blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked across the Gulf of Maine during the overnight hours of the 19th into the 20th. The low then lifted northeast across New Brunswick during the 20th.  The low brought heavy snow to northern and eastern Maine. Snow expanded northeast across the region through the late morning and afternoon hours of the 19th. Snow...heavy at times...then occurred through the evening hours of the 19th into the morning of the 20th. Warning criteria snow totals were met through the evening hours of the 19th into the early morning hours of the 20th. The snow wound down across much of the region through the morning of the 20th...lingering across extreme northern and northeastern areas into the early afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations across Downeast areas generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches...with localized totals up to 15 inches. Storm totals across central and northern areas generally ranged from 9 to 15 inches...with localized totals up to 19 inches. The heaviest accumulations generally occurred from northern Penobscot to southeast Aroostook counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 8 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed rapidly off the Carolina coast late on the 7th and early on the 8th.  The storm moved very slowly northeast during the 8th and 9th as it continued to intensify.  By the morning of the 10th, the storm was located just to the east of Nova Scotia.  The storm brought heavy snow, high winds, and blizzard conditions to the state.  Snowfall amounts were generally 18 inches or more in coastal and interior zones where blizzard conditions caused considerable blowing and drifting snow.  In the mountains and foothills, snowfall amounts were in the 4 to 18 inch range.  Most of coastal Maine had blizzard conditions for 9 to 12 hours with interior areas reporting blizzard conditions for 3 to 10 hours.

The combination of snowmelt (due to warm weather) and 1 to 1.5 inches of rain caused the Carrabassett River to rise. The rising water caused an ice jam which resulted in a several hours of minor flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmelt and heavy rain caused an ice jam on the Carrabassett River at North Anson (flood stage 15.0 ft), which crested at 15.41 ft and resulted in several hours of minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking across northern Maine drew a cold front across the region during the afternoon hours of the 16th...with thunderstorms developing in advance of the front. Severe thunderstorms produced large hail across portions of Washintgton county.

An arctic cold front crossed the region during the afternoon of the 20th bringing the coldest air of the season to northern and eastern Maine. The arctic air then remained into the 27th. The coldest temperatures of the arctic outbreak occurred during the morning of the 22nd. Low temperatures on the morning of the 22nd generally ranged from 25 to 35 below zero across northwest areas...with 15 to 25 below zero across much of the remainder of northern Maine...to zero to 5 above Downeast. The coldest recorded temperature of the outbreak...37 below zero at Big Black River...occurred during the morning of the 22nd. Low temperatures on the morning of the 23rd generally ranged from 15 to 25 below zero across northern areas...to zero to 10 below Downeast. High temperatures from the 22nd through the 24th struggled to reach zero across northern areas...with generally single digits Downeast. Low temperatures from the 24th to the 27th varied with the extent of cloud cover. Gusty winds occasionally produced dangerous wind chills during the outbreak with values of 45 to 55 below zero at times across northern areas and 25 to 35 below zero Downeast. Wind Chill Advisories and Warnings were issued at various times during the outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arctic air settled across the region through the week of the 20th to 27th. Winds occasionally combined with the arctic air to produce dangerous wind chills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure lifted north across Maine during the 27th. A tight pressure gradient between high pressure to the east and the low produced strong southerly winds across Washington and Hancock counties. The strong winds occurred from mid morning into the evening hours. The strongest winds occurred along the coast with  onshore winds where gusts approached 70 mph. The strong winds brought down trees and power lines across Washington and Hancock counties with the most significant impacts along the coast. Around 5000 customers were without power at the peak of the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure lifted north across Maine during the 27th. A tight pressure gradient between high pressure to the east and the low produced strong southerly winds across Washington and Hancock counties. The strong winds occurred from mid morning into the evening hours. The strongest winds occurred along the coast with  onshore winds where gusts approached 70 mph. The strong winds brought down trees and power lines across Washington and Hancock counties with the most significant impacts along the coast. Around 5000 customers were without power at the peak of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 55 to 65 mph...brought down trees and power lines. Widespread damage to trees and power lines was reported across the region including the towns of Castine...Deer Isle...Stonington and Blue Hill.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified as it rapidly moved up the coast during the early morning hours of December 15th. Storm force winds and large nearshore waves topping out at 20 feet coincided with high tide. Significant erosion and splash-over occurred in Saco, Maine. The water surrounded a community home and closed a street due to flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm force winds combined with 20 foot seas to cause coastal flooding at the time of high tide in coastal York County. Significant erosion and splash-over occured in Saco where water surrounded a community home and closed a street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic cold front crossed the region during the afternoon of the 20th bringing the coldest air of the season to northern and eastern Maine. The arctic air then remained into the 27th. The coldest temperatures of the arctic outbreak occurred during the morning of the 22nd. Low temperatures on the morning of the 22nd generally ranged from 25 to 35 below zero across northwest areas...with 15 to 25 below zero across much of the remainder of northern Maine...to zero to 5 above Downeast. The coldest recorded temperature of the outbreak...37 below zero at Big Black River...occurred during the morning of the 22nd. Low temperatures on the morning of the 23rd generally ranged from 15 to 25 below zero across northern areas...to zero to 10 below Downeast. High temperatures from the 22nd through the 24th struggled to reach zero across northern areas...with generally single digits Downeast. Low temperatures from the 24th to the 27th varied with the extent of cloud cover. Gusty winds occasionally produced dangerous wind chills during the outbreak with values of 45 to 55 below zero at times across northern areas and 25 to 35 below zero Downeast. Wind Chill Advisories and Warnings were issued at various times during the outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Arctic air settled across the region through the week of the 20th to 27th. Winds occasionally combined with the arctic air to produce dangerous wind chills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure tracked northwest of Maine during the 1st drawing a cold front across the region. A strong low level jet crossed the region in advance of the front through the afternoon and evening of the 1st. Winds were sustained at speeds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of 50 to 60 mph. Temperatures during the late morning and early afternoon warmed into the 60s and lower 70s across southwest Maine. An isolated cell moving through York County downed several trees on wires in North Berwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late summer surge of heat and humidity enveloped much of the northeast on the 11th of September. Afternoon temperatures climbed into the lower to mid 90s with dew points in the lower 70s resulting in high CAPE values throughout the forecast area. In addition, a very strong low to mid level jet was in place across the region producing a highly sheared environment. Late afternoon convection developing over western New England quickly formed into a large squall line with numerous bowing segments. This line of storms produced numerous reports of wind damage and large hail as it crossed the forecast area through the late evening hours before pushing offshore shortly before midnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 0.88 inch hail in Winthrop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the region during the morning and early afternoon of October 7th. By mid afternoon much of New Hampshire and western Maine had broken into the warm sector with temperatures pushing into the lower to  mid 70s. A strong cold front approaching from the west set the stage for late afternoon convection in a highly sheared environment. Damage associated with these storms was exclusively due to strong winds as a squall line with bowing segments moved through the region during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed numerous trees and power lines throughout Rangely.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late summer surge of heat and humidity enveloped much of the northeast on the 11th of September. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low level jet crossed the region in advance of a cold front through the afternoon hours of the 7th into the early morning hours of the 8th. Convective line segments...mostly without thunder...also developed in the vicinity of the front which helped mix the stronger winds aloft to the surface. Wind gusts generally ranged from 35 to 45 mph...though locally higher gusts up to around 60 mph occurred with the convective line segments. The downed trees and power lines occurred with the convective line segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was toppled on power lines by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in 3 hours sending the Swift River at Roxbury (flood stage 7.0 ft), above flood stage. The river crested at 8.03 feet after a brief period of minor flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon hours of the 19th in advance of an approaching cold front. The storms then persisted into the evening. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds. A tornado also occurred in northern Baxter State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the morning of the 11th allowing unseasonably warm and humid air to overspread the region. An upper level disturbance also began to approach during the evening hours. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours then continued into the evening producing an unusual late season severe weather event. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail. A tornado also briefly touched down in northern Piscataquis county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were toppled or snapped by wind gusts estimated at 70 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the morning of the 11th allowing unseasonably warm and humid air to overspread the region. An upper level disturbance also began to approach during the evening hours. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours then continued into the evening producing an unusual late season severe weather event. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail. A tornado also briefly touched down in northern Piscataquis county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A high precipitation supercell thunderstorm produced a brief tornado which caused tree damage in the North Maine Woods. Maximum winds were estimated at around 80 mph...with an average path width around 100 yards. The storm which produced the tornado then quickly became outflow dominated and produced extensive straight line wind damage to trees further east-southeast along the path of the storm. Estimates were based on an aircraft survey of the damage path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the morning of the 11th allowing unseasonably warm and humid air to overspread the region. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail. A tornado also briefly touched down in northern Piscataquis county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive tree damage...with trees toppled and snapped...occurred from wind gusts estimated at 80 mph along the path of the storm. Vehicles were trapped along Route 11 due to the fallen trees. This wind damage was caused by the same storm which had produced the tornado in northern Piscataquis county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low and cold pool aloft crossed the region during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 4th. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced large hail across northern portions of Aroostook county during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail ranging from 0.50 inch up to 1.00 inch was reported in Woodland. The hail accumulated up to 3 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and very humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of August 28th. There was little in the way of shear and upper dynamics so storms which formed were very slow moving and produced very heavy rain in a short period of time. One particular storm which formed on the sea breeze front dumped 2 to 4 inches of rain in the Waterville area of Kennebec County resulting in flooding of many streets in town.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in less than 2 hours resulting in flooding of many streets in Waterville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front dropping south through the region was the focus for afternoon and evening convection. High precipitable water values combined with weak shear to produced slow moving thunderstorms and very heavy rain. An area of thunderstorms training over the Hiram area of Oxford County produced 2 to 3 inches of rain in 2 hours causing road flooding and washouts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms training over the Hiram area of Oxford County produced 2 to 3 inches of rain in 2 hours resulting in road flooding and washouts.

A cold front sweeping through the region on the afternoon of August 2nd was the focus for convection. A few of these storms reached severe limits producing mainly wind damage although some small hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm broke a utility pole downing wires in Mercer.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Poland.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed several utility poles and numerous trees in Harrison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharpening upper level trough was swinging through the Northeast on the afternoon of August 4th. Good dynamics associated with this system along with rapid cooling aloft produced afternoon showers and some thunderstorms. Freezing levels dropped during the afternoon and some of the stronger cells produced hail. One isolated cell produced 1 inch hail in York County before pushing offshore.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe thunderstorm produced 1 inch hail near Kennebunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north across the region during the morning of the 11th allowing unseasonably warm and humid air to overspread the region. An upper level disturbance also began to approach during the evening hours. Severe thunderstorms developed during the late afternoon hours then continued into the evening producing an unusual late season severe weather event. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail. A tornado also briefly touched down in northern Piscataquis county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time is estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were toppled by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across the region during the afternoon hours of the 19th in advance of an approaching cold front. The storms then persisted into the evening. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds. A tornado also occurred in northern Baxter State Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado with estimated wind speeds of around 90 mph produced extensive tree damage (DI 27, DI 28 DOD4). The tornado was embedded within an area of straight line winds with speeds estimated at up to 75 mph which produced extensive tree damage along an 18 mile path. The average path width of the tornado was around 150 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper disturbance crossed the region during the afternoon and evening hours of the 17th. Severe thunderstorms developed across eastern Maine in advance of the front during the late afternoon hours...then persisted into the evening. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds...along with a brief tornado in the Danforth area.  Heavy rain produced localized flash flooding in the Caribou area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado...embedded within an area of damaging straight-line winds...briefly touched down destroying a metal shed (DI 1, DOD5). Based on the damage...the tornado winds were estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracking north across Maine brought very heavy rain to Washington county during the 26th. Rainfall totals of 3.50 to 5.00 inches were common across much of the county...with localized totals to around 6.00 inches. The most significant flash flooding occurred in the Calais area...with lesser flash flooding across the remainder of the county. Flash flooding occurred in the Calais area during the evening hours. Some roads were closed with other roads washed out or damaged to varying degrees. Several buildings were also flooded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding around the Calais area washed out and damaged roads. Several buildings were also flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low and cold pool aloft crossed the region during the late afternoon and evening hours of the 4th. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced large hail across northern portions of Aroostook county during the late afternoon and evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour produced localized flash flooding in the New Sweden area. Six inches of water were reported flowing across portions of Westmanland Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the region during the morning and early afternoon of October 7th. By mid afternoon much of New Hampshire and western Maine had broken into the warm sector with temperatures pushing into the lower to  mid 70s. A strong cold front approaching from the west set the stage for late afternoon convection in a highly sheared environment. Damage associated with these storms was exclusively due to strong winds as a squall line with bowing segments moved through the region during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large limbs on wires on Old South Road in South Berwick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front approached Maine during the afternoon of June 2nd...while a pre-frontal trof and upper level disturbance crossed the region. Severe thunderstorms developed across northern and eastern Maine during the early afternoon hours of the 2nd...then persisted into the evening. Some locations experienced several rounds of severe weather through the afternoon and evening with damaging winds and large hail. Heavy rain produced flash flooding in spots...particularly in areas which experienced several thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled along Route 11 by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front combined with strong wind fields and high CAPES over the region resulting in a widespread severe thunderstorm outbreak during the afternoon and evening hours of June 2nd. These storms produced mainly wind damage with isolated reports of large hail and some flash flooding.  A weak tornado touched down in Pleasant Ridge Plantation.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees on Dunbar Hill and Crosstown Roads in Embden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of the 25th in advance of a cold front moving south across the region. Some of the thunderstorms became severe across east-central and Downeast areas...producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were toppled along Route 6...east of Lincoln...by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed in advance of a stalling cold front during the afternoon hours of the 1st. Isolated severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds across portions of Piscataquis county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and utility poles were toppled in Greenville by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon of September 12th. Strong shear was present as a cold front moved into the region from the west during the early afternoon. Large hail and damaging winds were reported with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large branches in Eliot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours of the 25th in advance of a cold front moving south across the region. Some of the thunderstorms became severe across east-central and Downeast areas...producing damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled in Corinth by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The falling tree brought down 3 power poles.

Severe thunderstorms developed across northern and eastern Maine during the early afternoon hours of the 2nd...then persisted into the evening. Some locations experienced several rounds of severe weather through the afternoon and evening with damaging winds and large hail. Heavy rain produced flash flooding in spots...particularly in areas which experienced several thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled in Shin Pond by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north through the region during the day on the 19th of July, leaving the forecast area in a very hot and humid air mass. A few thunderstorms along this boundary became severe during the morning hours but bulk of the severe storms came during the evening hours. Thunderstorms riding over an upper level ridge along this boundary developed during the late afternoon across eastern Ontario and southern Quebec. These storm intensified as they dropped southeast through the evening hours. Damage from these storms was exclusively due to strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed multiple trees in Madison.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front sweeping through the region on the afternoon of August 2nd was the focus for convection. A few of these storms reached severe limits producing mainly wind damage although some small hail was also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Harrison.

A strong cold front approaching from the west set the stage for late afternoon convection in a highly sheared environment. Damage associated with these storms was exclusively due to strong winds as a squall line with bowing segments moved through the region during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees blocking East Andover Road in Andover.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A hot and humid airmass was in place across the region on the afternoon and evening of August 28th. Although shear was weak across the region, a sea breeze front pushed inland during the late afternoon and early evening producing enough forcing to initiate convection in the foothills of western Maine. High precipitable water values resulted in slow moving thunderstorms with very heavy rain, some of which produced wet microbursts. Thunderstorm wind damage and some flash flooding were reported with stronger cells.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed large branches on power lines resulting in power outages in Readfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very hot and humid air mass remained entrenched across the forecast area on the 20th of July. An approaching cold front set the stage for more severe thunderstorms across the region. Once again elevated freezing levels inhibited development of large hail and all reports of severe weather involved wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a tree on Field Road in Searsport.
